title: Why we still phone our parents at the same hour
summary: Describe a small, repeated habit you have of staying in contact with a family member, and explore what that habit reveals about your relationship.
topic: Why we still phone our parents at the same hour
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) describing a regular habit of staying in touch with a parent or close family member, and reflecting on what that habit means and what would happen if you stopped. Use at least one relative clause, a conditional sentence, the present perfect for continuity, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: Every Sunday evening, without really planning it, I find myself picking up the phone and calling…
It started during a difficult period when I needed to hear a familiar voice, and somehow it never stopped…
If you asked me why I still call at that particular hour, I would probably struggle to give you a precise reason, but…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thói quen, công việc thường ngày
sự trấn an, cảm giác yên tâm
cảm giác liên tục, không gián đoạn
giúp ai đó cảm thấy ổn định
không được nói ra, ngầm hiểu
mất đi sợi dây kết nối
sẵn sàng, có thể tiếp cận
đường dài, xa cách địa lý
coi điều gì đó là hiển nhiên

sampleText:
Every Sunday evening, somewhere between seven and eight, I call my mother. It has been this way for about six years, ever since I moved to a different city for work. There was no agreement about the time — it simply settled into that slot because Sunday evenings were when she finished cooking and I had no other reason to rush. The calls last between twenty and forty minutes, and we talk about nothing in particular: a television programme she is watching, a problem I had at work, something one of my cousins said. Neither of us would describe these calls as important. And yet if I miss one, even for a reasonable reason like travel, I notice the gap for several days afterward.

Although I am aware that calling my mother every Sunday is a small and ordinary thing, I have started to think about what it actually does. It gives both of us a point on the week that holds steady regardless of what else is happening. My mother, who retired two years ago and lives alone in the house where I grew up, does not always have a structured week. The Sunday call gives her something to expect and me something to deliver. However, the call is not just a service — it is a form of staying known to each other. Without those weekly conversations, we would gradually lose the texture of each other's daily lives: the small frustrations, the minor hopes, the weather in each other's cities. Relationships maintained only through visits and special occasions tend to become formal over time. It is the ordinary repetition that keeps them alive.

I have sometimes wondered what would happen if the habit simply ended — if one Sunday I forgot and neither of us mentioned it the following week. I think we would recover, but something would shift, and the shift would be hard to name. What I have realised is that the habit is not really about information. It is about the quiet, ongoing confirmation that we are still present in each other's lives. Friends I know in Seoul and in São Paulo have described nearly identical habits with their own parents: a call on a particular day, at a roughly consistent time, about nothing much in particular. The content is almost beside the point. What matters is the act of showing up, week after week, without needing a reason other than the fact that it has always been done.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for continuity over time
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It has been this way for about six years, ever since I moved to a different city' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'for' và 'ever since' để diễn tả một tình trạng bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn còn tiếp tục — nhấn mạnh tính liên tục của thói quen.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It has been this way for about six years, ever since I moved to a different city' uses the present perfect with 'for' and 'ever since' to describe a state that started in the past and is still continuing — emphasising the continuity of the habit.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for imagined loss
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Without those weekly conversations, we would gradually lose the texture of each other's daily lives' dùng 'would + nguyên thể' để diễn đạt kết quả giả định nếu thói quen biến mất — đây là điều kiện loại 2 dùng 'without' thay vì 'if' để thêm phần tự nhiên và tinh tế.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Without those weekly conversations, we would gradually lose the texture of each other's daily lives' uses 'would + base verb' to express a hypothetical outcome if the habit disappeared — a second conditional using 'without' instead of 'if' for a more natural, nuanced effect.

sampleTranslationVi:
Mỗi tối Chủ nhật, đâu đó giữa bảy và tám giờ, tôi gọi điện cho mẹ. Điều này đã như vậy khoảng sáu năm, kể từ khi tôi chuyển đến một thành phố khác để làm việc. Không có thỏa thuận về thời gian — nó đơn giản là đã ổn định vào khoảng thời gian đó vì tối Chủ nhật là lúc bà ấy nấu xong và tôi không có lý do gì phải vội vàng. Các cuộc gọi kéo dài từ hai mươi đến bốn mươi phút, và chúng tôi nói về không có gì đặc biệt: một chương trình truyền hình bà đang xem, một vấn đề tôi gặp ở công việc, điều gì đó một người anh em họ của tôi nói. Không ai trong chúng tôi mô tả những cuộc gọi này là quan trọng. Và tuy nhiên, nếu tôi bỏ lỡ một cuộc, dù vì lý do hợp lý như đi du lịch, tôi vẫn nhận thấy sự thiếu hụt trong nhiều ngày sau đó.

Mặc dù tôi biết rằng việc gọi cho mẹ mỗi Chủ nhật là điều nhỏ bé và bình thường, tôi đã bắt đầu suy nghĩ về những gì nó thực sự mang lại. Nó cho cả hai chúng tôi một điểm trong tuần duy trì ổn định bất kể điều gì khác đang xảy ra. Mẹ tôi, người đã về hưu hai năm trước và sống một mình trong ngôi nhà nơi tôi lớn lên, không phải lúc nào cũng có một tuần có cấu trúc. Cuộc gọi tối Chủ nhật mang lại cho bà điều gì đó để mong đợi và tôi điều gì đó để thực hiện. Tuy nhiên, cuộc gọi không chỉ là một dịch vụ — đó là một cách để tiếp tục được biết đến bởi nhau. Không có những cuộc trò chuyện hàng tuần đó, chúng tôi dần dần sẽ mất đi sắc thái của cuộc sống hàng ngày của nhau: những bực bội nhỏ, những hy vọng bình thường, thời tiết ở mỗi thành phố. Các mối quan hệ chỉ được duy trì qua những lần thăm hỏi và dịp đặc biệt có xu hướng trở nên trang trọng theo thời gian. Chính sự lặp lại thông thường giữ chúng tồn tại.

Đôi khi tôi tự hỏi điều gì sẽ xảy ra nếu thói quen đơn giản kết thúc — nếu một Chủ nhật nào đó tôi quên và không ai trong chúng tôi đề cập đến điều đó vào tuần sau. Tôi nghĩ chúng tôi sẽ phục hồi, nhưng điều gì đó sẽ thay đổi, và sự thay đổi đó sẽ khó mà đặt tên. Điều tôi nhận ra là thói quen này không thực sự về thông tin. Đó là về sự xác nhận yên lặng, liên tục rằng chúng tôi vẫn hiện diện trong cuộc sống của nhau. Bạn bè tôi quen biết ở Seoul và São Paulo đã mô tả những thói quen gần như giống nhau với cha mẹ của chính họ: một cuộc gọi vào một ngày cụ thể, vào một thời gian tương đối nhất quán, về không có gì nhiều đặc biệt. Nội dung gần như không phải điểm chính. Điều quan trọng là hành động xuất hiện, tuần này qua tuần khác, mà không cần lý do nào khác ngoài việc nó đã luôn được thực hiện như vậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: My mother, which retired two years ago, lives alone in the house where I grew up.
My mother, who retired two years ago, lives alone in the house where I grew up.
My mother, who she retired two years ago, lives alone in the house where I grew up.
My mother, that retired two years ago, lives alone in the house where I grew up.
mcQuestions.explanation: Use 'who' for people in relative clauses; 'which' is for things; do not repeat the subject with 'she'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Who' introduces a non-defining relative clause for people; commas show it adds extra information.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect with 'for' correctly?
options: It has been this way since about six years.
It is this way for about six years.
It has been this way for about six years.
It was this way for about six years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect + 'for' describes a state lasting from the past to now; 'since' is used with a specific starting point, not a duration.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'for' with a period of time; use 'since' with a specific start point.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly for concession?
options: Although I am aware that calling my mother is a small thing but I have started to think about what it does.
Although, I am aware that calling my mother is a small thing, I have started to think about what it does.
I am aware that calling my mother is although a small thing, I have started to think about what it does.
Although I am aware that calling my mother is a small thing, I have started to think about what it does.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'although', no 'but' in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces a concessive clause; never add 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly to introduce a contrast?
options: However, the call is not just a service — it is a form of staying known to each other.
However the call is not just a service — it is a form of staying known to each other.
The call however, is not just a service — it is a form of staying known to each other.
The call is not just a service however — it is a form of staying known to each other.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However' used as a sentence adverb needs a comma after it at the start of a sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: When 'however' begins a sentence as a contrast adverb, it must be followed by a comma.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a second conditional correctly to describe a hypothetical loss?
options: Without those calls, we will gradually lose the texture of each other's daily lives.
Without those calls, we would gradually lose the texture of each other's daily lives.
Without those calls, we would gradually lost the texture of each other's daily lives.
Without those calls, we gradually would lose the texture of each other's daily lives.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional with 'without': 'without + noun phrase, would + base verb'; 'would' must precede the base form, not the past form.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Without + noun phrase' can replace 'if not'; the main clause uses 'would + base verb'.

options: Relationships maintained only through visits tend becoming formal over time.
Relationships maintained only through visits tends to become formal over time.
Relationships maintained only through visits tend to become formal over time.
Relationships maintained only through visits are tending to become formal over time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + base verb' is correct; the subject 'relationships' is plural, so 'tend' (not 'tends') is used.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tend to + base verb'; plural subjects need 'tend', not 'tends'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'keep in touch' in the present perfect?
options: We have kept in the touch every Sunday for six years.
We have kept in touch every Sunday since six years.
We have been keeping in touch every Sunday since six years.
We have kept in touch every Sunday for six years.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Keep in touch' is a fixed phrase (no article); use 'for' with a duration ('six years'), not 'since'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Keep in touch' has no article; use 'for' with a duration, 'since' with a starting point.

options: It holds steady regardless of what else is happening.
It holds steady regardless what else is happening.
It holds steady regardless to what else is happening.
It holds steady regardless about what else is happening.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Regardless of' is the fixed phrase; 'regardless' alone without 'of', or with other prepositions, is incorrect.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Regardless of' is always followed by 'of'; no other preposition is used.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'take for granted' correctly?
options: We should not take for granted these small habits.
We should not take these small habits for granted.
We should not take these small habits granted.
We should not take granted these small habits.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take something for granted' is the fixed phrase; the object usually comes between 'take' and 'for granted'.
mcQuestions.hint: The object goes between 'take' and 'for granted': 'take these habits for granted'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'ever since' with the present perfect?
options: It is this way ever since I moved to a different city.
It was this way ever since I moved to a different city.
It has been this way ever since I moved to a different city.
It has been this way ever since I have moved to a different city.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Ever since + past simple' is the standard structure; after 'since', use past simple for the starting event, and present perfect in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'ever since', use past simple for the starting event; present perfect in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'make time for'?
options: She always makes the time for the weekly call, even when she is busy.
She always makes time to the weekly call, even when she is busy.
She always makes time at the weekly call, even when she is busy.
She always makes time for the weekly call, even when she is busy.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make time for' is the correct collocation meaning to find time in a schedule; no article 'the' before 'time', and the preposition is 'for'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Make time for' = to find time for; no article before 'time'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is a habit of regular contact a sign of a strong relationship, or can it become a substitute for deeper communication — and how would you know the difference?
